What Is Inductive Bias?
Inductive bias:
The assumptions a model makes about the structure of data before seeing any training examples.
All models have inductive bias.
Linear regression → assumes linear relationship
MLP → assumes no spatial structure
CNN → assumes local correlations and translation consistency
Images are structured 2D signals:
Local correlation
Repeated patterns (edges, textures)
Spatial hierarchy
CNNs encode spatial inductive bias through:
Local connectivity
Weight sharing
Translation consistency
This reduces parameters and improves generalization.
43
Good generalization happens when the model’s inductive bias matches the true structure of the data.
If data is images → spatial bias helps
If data is time series → temporal bias helps

44
Hierarchical Feature Learning in CNNs

1×1 Convolution
What Does 1×1 Convolution Do?
Performs channel mixing
Earlier layers detect: Edges, Textures, Colors, Simple patterns
1×1 convolution learns how to combine them.
Example: Combine “horizontal edge” + “skin color” → “face feature”
So, it learns interactions between feature maps.
Reduces dimensionality
Less computation
Less memory
Lower overfitting risk
Adds non-linearity
Performs linear projection
Followed by non-linear activation
Enables bottleneck architectures (e.g.,
ResNet
).

Discussion 1:
When Is Accuracy Misleading?
In which of the following scenarios would a
high accuracy
value suggest that the ML model is doing a good job?
A deadly, but curable, medical condition afflicts .01% of the population. An ML model uses symptoms as features and predicts this affliction with an accuracy of 99.99%.
An expensive robotic chicken crosses a very busy road a thousand times per day. An ML model evaluates traffic patterns and predicts when this chicken can safely cross the street with an accuracy of 99.99%.
In the game of roulette, a ball is dropped on a spinning wheel and eventually lands in one of 38 slots. Using visual features (the spin of the ball, the position of the wheel when the ball was dropped, the height of the ball over the wheel), an ML model can predict the slot that the ball will land in with an accuracy of 4%.

If Model A has better precision than model B, then model A is better.
If model A has better sensitivity (recall) than model B, then model A is better.

High recall is essential for safety.

possible_slide_title: Discussion 5: What Does AUC Really Measure?
visible_text:
Discussion 5:
What Does AUC Really Measure?
How would multiplying all of the predictions from a given model by 2.0 change the model's performance as measured by AUC?
(for example, if the model predicts 0.4, we multiply by 2.0 to get a prediction of 0.8)
It would make AUC better, because the prediction values are all farther apart.
No change. AUC only cares about relative prediction scores.
It would make AUC terrible, since the prediction values are now way off.
29

possible_slide_title: Discussion 5 solution: What Does AUC Really Measure?
visible_text:
Discussion 5 solution:
What Does AUC Really Measure?
How would multiplying all of the predictions from a given model by 2.0 change the model's performance as measured by AUC?
(for example, if the model predicts 0.4, we multiply by 2.0 to get a prediction of 0.8)
It would make AUC better, because the prediction values are all farther apart.
No change. AUC only cares about relative prediction scores.
It would make AUC terrible, since the prediction values are now way off.
AUC depends only on ranking, not absolute values.
If predictions are multiplied by 2: Relative ordering unchanged → ROC unchanged → AUC unchanged
30

board Artifacts
40
Uneven overlap can create periodic texture patterns in the output.
The problem is most visible when stride and kernel size interact poorly.
A reliable mitigation is interpolation or resize followed by a regular convolution.

Why Standard RNNs Struggle with Long-Term Dependencies
Short-Term Dependencies
Relevant information is nearby.
Standard RNNs can often learn it
Example: Predict the last word in
“the clouds are in the
sky
,”
8
Long-Term Dependencies
Relevant information is far away
Gradients may vanish over many time steps
Learning becomes difficult
Example: Understanding a word or event much earlier in a sentence or document
Standard RNNs often struggle when useful information must travel across many time steps. Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) was designed to address this problem.

Dot Product is a Similarity Score
If two vectors are identical → Cosine similarity = 1.0
If two vectors are unrelated (perpendicular) → Cosine similarity = 0.0
If two vectors are opposite → Cosine similarity = -1.0

Sinusoidal Positional Encoding: Basic Idea
Each token position is represented by a deterministic vector built from sine and cosine functions at different frequencies.
37
https://arxiv.org/abs/1706.03762
……

Sinusoidal Positional Encoding: Results
Each token position is represented by a deterministic vector built from sine and cosine functions at different frequencies.
38
Low-frequency dimensions capture coarse position; high-frequency dimensions capture fine position.

Intuition for Sinusoidal Positional Encoding
Different dimensions use sine and cosine waves with different frequencies, so each position gets a distinct pattern.
Nearby positions have similar encodings
Different frequencies capture both local and global position patterns
The encoding extends naturally to unseen positions
Sinusoidal encoding gives each position a unique, multi-scale signature.
39

Sinusoidal Positional Encoding: Visualization
How Sinusoidal Encoding Represents Relative Position
Nearby positions have similar encodings
Different frequencies capture both small and large offsets
Relative distance is reflected in how the encoding changes across positions
Attention can use these patterns to reason about token order
Relative position is reflected by how the sinusoidal pattern changes from one token to the next.

Multiple heads provide multiple views of the same sequence.

Causal Masking in GPT
At position 4, GPT can attend only to the current token and earlier tokens. This masking prevents access to future tokens.

possible_slide_title: Core Idea of ViT: Turn an Image into a Sequence of Tokens
visible_text:
4
Core Idea of
ViT
: Turn an Image into a Sequence of Tokens
An Image is Worth 16x16 Words: Transformers for Image Recognition at Scale,
Dosovitskiy
et al., ICLR 2021
https://arxiv.org/abs/2010.11929
ViT
treats image patches as tokens, similar to words in NLP.

VIT Architecture
Overview
5
Split an image into fixed-size patches
Treat patches as tokens and feed them to a Transformer encoder
Global self-attention captures long-range relationships across the image
ViT
shows that pure Transformers can work for vision.

Inductive Bias
Inductive bias
is the built-in assumption a model uses to generalize beyond the training data
It helps the model decide
what patterns are likely to matter
before seeing enough data
Without inductive bias, learning would require much more data and computation
6
Stronger inductive bias can make learning easier; weaker inductive bias can make the model more general but more data-hungry.
CNN:
strong inductive bias
locality
translation equivariance
hierarchical feature extraction
ViT
: weaker inductive bias
fewer built-in assumptions about images
more flexible, but often needs more data or stronger pretraining

possible_slide_title: Why Vanilla ViT Tokenization Is Too Crude
visible_text:
Why Vanilla
ViT
Tokenization Is Too Crude
ViT
splits an image into
fixed-size patches
in one step
This hard split is simple, but it weakens local continuity across patch boundaries.
As a result, vanilla
ViT
has:
weaker inductive bias
than CNNs
Inductive bias
= the built-in assumptions that guide how a model learns and generalizes.
lower sample efficiency
Sample efficiency
means how well a model learns from a limited amount of training data.
stronger dependence on
very large pretraining datasets
33
Yuan et al.,
Tokens-to-Token
ViT
: Training Vision Transformers from Scratch on ImageNet
, ICCV 2021
https://arxiv.org/abs/2101.11986
Vanilla
ViT
sees image patches, but does not naturally build local visual structure.

T2T-ViT: Progressive Tokenization
T2T-ViT does not keep the initial tokens fixed.
It progressively re-forms tokens from neighboring tokens.
This adds local structure while shortening the token sequence over stages.
34
T2T-ViT makes
ViTs
more data-efficient and more vision-friendly by injecting local structure into tokenization.

Soft Split = Overlapping Tokenization
T2T-ViT: progressive tokenization with overlap
Benefit: better local structure and better sample efficiency
35
T2T-ViT makes
ViTs
more data-efficient and more vision-friendly by injecting local structure into tokenization.
How Soft Split Works (Step-by-Step)
Start with an image (or reconstructed feature map)
Slide a
k × k window
across the image
Use
stride < k
→ creates overlap
For each window:
Flatten it → becomes a token
Output = a new sequence of tokens

Pyramid Vision Transformer (PVT)
Vanilla
ViT
keeps the same token resolution throughout the network.
PVT instead builds a hierarchical pyramid of features, like a CNN.
This design is better matched to vision tasks that need multi-scale representations.
36
Wang et al., Pyramid Vision Transformer: A Versatile Backbone for Dense Prediction without Convolutions, ICCV 2021
https://arxiv.org/abs/2102.12122

Pyramid Vision Transformer (PVT):
Achitecture
Builds a multi-scale pyramid of features (like a CNN).
37
Wang et al., Pyramid Vision Transformer: A Versatile Backbone for Dense Prediction without Convolutions, ICCV 2021
https://arxiv.org/abs/2102.12122
PVT =
ViT
+ CNN-style feature pyramid for dense vision tasks.
Spatial-Reduction Attention

possible_slide_title: Spatial-Reduction Attention (SRA): Keep Q, Reduce K and V
visible_text:
Spatial-Reduction Attention (SRA): Keep Q, Reduce K and V
Standard attention is expensive at high resolution
SRA reduces cost by compressing K and V only
SRA produces the same output shape as standard attention (H × W × C).
38
Key Ides:
Q: full resolution (fine-grained)
K, V:
downsampled
(coarse summary)
implemented via
strided
conv or patch grouping
Each query attends to a compressed global context
Example:
56×56 → 3136 tokens
R=4 → K,V = 14×14 = 196 tokens
SRA keeps full-resolution outputs while reducing computation by compressing K and V.
Standard Attention
SRA
Q, K, V: H × W
Q: H × W
K, V: H/R × W/R

Swin Transformer: Hierarchical
ViT
Starts with small 4×4 patches
Computes self-attention within local windows
Shifts the windows in the next layer to connect neighboring regions
Builds a hierarchical representation by merging patches across stages
Liu et al, Swin Transformer: Hierarchical Vision Transformer using Shifted Windows, CVPR 2021
https://arxiv.org/abs/2103.14030
Swin adds locality and hierarchy while keeping Transformer flexibility.
39

Comparing Early Vision Transformer Variants
ViT
: pure global attention
DeiT
: better training with limited data
T2T-ViT
: better tokenization and local structure
PVT / Swin
: hierarchical design for dense prediction
59
Most improvements target one of three issues: data efficiency, locality, or scalability.

Vision transformers evolved from image classifiers into foundation encoders for representation learning, multimodal understanding, and generation.

Summary: How Vision Transformers Evolved
Images can be split into patches and treated as tokens
The main challenges are efficiency, locality, and data requirements
Later models improved training, hierarchy, and self-supervised learning
Vision Transformers now support classification, segmentation, and multimodal understanding
71
Transformers for vision became powerful by combining patch-based modeling with better inductive structure and better pretraining.
ViT
→ better training → better tokenization → hierarchical designs → self-supervised learning → multimodal models.

The

GAN learning happens through this competition

Discriminator: classify real as 1, fake as 0
Generator: make fake be classified as 1

GAN: Strengths and Weaknesses
Weaknesses
Harder and less stable to train
Does not answer inference questions such as 𝑝(𝑥) or 𝑝(𝑧∣𝑥)
Training details often need tuning; there is no single best rule for update balance
16
Strengths
Generates highly realistic samples
Learns through a two-player game between generator and discriminator
Focuses directly on sampling, without defining an explicit density function
GANs can generate highly realistic samples, but they are harder to train and less interpretable than likelihood-based models.
GAN research has focused heavily on better loss functions, more stable training, and extending GANs to conditional generation and other applications.

Diffusion Models
17
Gradually add noise to data
Learn to reverse the noising process
Generate new samples from noise
Denoise step by step
Use the score to move toward cleaner, more likely data
Forward diffusion creates noisy training inputs; the model learns the reverse denoising process
High-Resolution Image Synthesis with Latent Diffusion Models
, CVPR 2022

Latent Conditional Diffusion Model
18
High-Resolution Image Synthesis with Latent Diffusion Models
, CVPR 2022
Latent diffusion encodes an image into a compact latent space, denoises it step by step with a U-Net, and decodes the result back to pixel space.

visible_text:
Summary: Main Ideas of Generative Models
19
Autoencoders (AE)
learn compact representations by reconstructing the input
Variational Autoencoders (VAE)
make the latent space sampleable for generation
GANs
generate realistic samples through competition between generator and discriminator
Diffusion models
generate data by gradually reversing noise
Different generative models make different trade-offs in
sample quality, training stability, efficiency, and interpretability
